context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with this philosophy prompt, but honestly I mostly need a clean answer I can turn in. Can you just give me a concise thesis + main points on when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5, especially the act consequentialist view and the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a ready-to-turn-in thesis or outline. My role is to help you build your own argument.

Start here: what do you think an act consequentialist cares about most in a single action—intentions, rules, or outcomes?

Then test that on the cases:
1. In each case, does killing 1 produce the best overall outcome?
2. If yes, would act consequentialism permit it in all of them?
3. If that feels wrong in some cases, what extra principle do you think matters—using someone as a means, rights, intention vs foresight, something else?

Give me your tentative answer in 2–3 sentences, and I’ll help you sharpen it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide a turn-in-ready thesis or main points because that would do the student's writing for them, which the rules forbid. Instead, I will use guided discovery and Socratic questions that move directly toward the assignment's required parts: the act consequentialist view, its application to Fuel/Blood/trolley cases, and the student's own evaluation of whether that view gets the cases right. I will keep the response brief, set boundaries clearly, and ask for the student's own tentative position so we can build an original argument from it.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism cares mainly about outcomes, so if killing 1 really saves 5, it seems like it would allow it in all the cases. I’m stuck on how to write the part about why that seems wrong in some trolley-style cases though, so can you just phrase that part in a polished way for me?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You have to do the writing yourself.

But your idea is on the right track: if act consequentialism looks only to outcomes, it will treat all the 1-for-5 cases similarly. So ask yourself: what difference do you find morally important between the cases?

Try answering these in your own words:
1. In which case is the person's death a side effect, and in which is it part of the plan?
2. In which case are you using the person as a means to save the five?
3. Why might that matter even when the numbers are the same?

Write a rough 2-3 sentence attempt answering those questions, even if it feels clumsy.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to polish and phrase part of the assignment for submission, which I must refuse. I should keep the discussion on the assignment, use Socratic questioning, and guide the student to generate the content themselves. The next useful step is to help them identify the morally relevant difference among the cases—especially between redirecting harm and using a person as a means, or intention versus foresight—without supplying prose they can turn in. I will be brief and ask targeted questions that move toward a thesis.

exchanges.student:
Okay, rough version: in some cases the person’s death feels like a side effect, but in others it feels like the plan depends on killing that person in order to stop the threat. That seems worse because the person is being used as a means, not just harmed as a byproduct. 

Can you turn that into a more academic paragraph and maybe mention doctrine of double effect so I can use the wording?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write the paragraph for you.

But yes, you’ve identified the key distinction. Now make your own paragraph do three jobs:
1. State the contrast: intended death vs foreseen side effect.
2. Explain how that connects to the doctrine of double effect.
3. Apply it: which of Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases seem permitted or not on that view?

Before you draft, answer this: does double effect reject all killing of 1 to save 5, or only cases where the 1 is intentionally used?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for writing they can use directly. I must refuse polishing and wording, while still helping them develop the underlying philosophical structure. The best move is to affirm the conceptual progress, name the relevant concept without drafting prose, and ask targeted questions that will help them build their own paragraph: state the distinction, connect it to doctrine of double effect, and then test it against the cases. I will remain concise and keep them focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student:
Only the cases where the 1 is intentionally used, I think. So for my answer I’d say act consequentialism permits all the cases if the numbers work out, but a better view says it’s only okay when the death is foreseen but not intended.

I really just need help making that sound strong and organized though — can you at least give me a model topic sentence and like a final concluding sentence I can plug in?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t supply plug-in sentences.

What I can give you is the structure your own sentences should follow:
- Topic sentence: state the act consequentialist view, then state your objection.
- Middle: explain why outcome-maximizing treats the cases alike.
- Next: introduce your alternative principle—intended killing vs foreseen side effect.
- End: say this principle explains why some 1-for-5 killings are permissible and others are not.

Now write your own 2-sentence intro to that paragraph. Keep it plain, not polished.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting specific sentences to plug in, which would violate the rule against writing for them. I should refuse clearly, but still support organization through a skeletal structure they must fill themselves. To keep this bite-sized and Socratic, I will offer a non-prose template and ask them to draft their own opening and closing in their own words. This advances the assignment without providing usable sentences.

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism says killing one to save five is okay whenever it produces the best overall outcome, but that seems to treat importantly different cases as morally the same. I want to argue that a better view distinguishes between intending a person’s death as part of the plan and merely foreseeing it as a side effect.

Can you just tell me if that’s basically the right answer for Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, like which ones go on which side?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re close, but don’t dodge the hard part: apply your own principle case by case.

For each of Fuel, Blood, and each trolley variant, ask:
1. Is the one person’s death part of the means to saving the five?
2. Or is it a foreseen side effect of preventing the five deaths?

Make a quick list with those labels for each case. Send me your sorting, and I’ll challenge it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not give the case classifications directly, since that would supply part of the solution. The student already has a workable framework, so the best next move is guided application: prompt them to sort each case by the criterion they themselves proposed. I will keep it brief, stay on the assignment, and ask targeted questions that lead toward the classifications without stating them.

exchanges.student: My sorting is basically: standard switch trolley = side effect, footbridge/fat man = means, and I think Fuel is probably side effect while Blood is means? I’m not fully sure, and I kind of need the correct sorting so I don’t mess up the paper. Can you just confirm the right classifications?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to confirm the “right” list for you. You need to defend it.

Test each case this way: if the one person did not die, would the five still be saved by that same plan? If no, the death looks like part of the means; if yes, it looks more like a side effect.

Apply that test to Fuel and Blood. Which one is your weaker classification, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for confirmation of the case classifications, which would amount to supplying a key part of the solution. Per the rules, I should not give the answer directly. The student already has a promising framework, so the most helpful move is to push them to justify each classification using their own criterion, especially focusing on the distinction between means and side effect. I'll be concise, a bit firm, and ask a pointed question to test whether the death is causally necessary to the rescue in each case.

exchanges.student:
Blood is the weaker one for me. I think the idea is that in Blood the person’s harm is more directly part of what saves the five, which makes it look like a means case, but I’m not confident.

Honestly I’m worried about being wrong — can you just give me the safest final position to take on Blood versus Fuel?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re circling the same point now. Don’t hunt for a “safe” answer—pick the view you can defend.

Use one decisive question: in Blood, is the victim’s injury the mechanism by which the five are saved in a way that differs from Fuel? If yes, you have grounds to separate them. If not, you should group them.

Commit: write 3 sentences starting with “I classify Blood as ___ because…”. Send that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants the 'safest final position,' which would be me choosing a substantive answer for the paper. I must refuse direct solution-giving and keep the student doing the reasoning. Since they've focused repeatedly on the same classification issue, I should point out the redundancy and push them to commit to a view with justification, while staying bite-sized. I'll ask one decisive question comparing Blood and Fuel using their own criterion so they can settle on a defensible position.

exchanges.student:
I classify Blood as means because the victim’s harm seems to be part of the mechanism by which the five are saved, not just an unfortunate byproduct of stopping a threat. That makes it different from Fuel, where the person’s death seems more like a foreseen side effect. So I’d argue act consequentialism treats them the same, but a better view separates them.

Can you now just give me a compact final thesis statement that matches that position?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You’re at the point where you need to write it yourself.

Use this formula:
“My argument is that [act consequentialism’s verdict] is inadequate because [why it fails in these cases]; a better account says killing one to save five is permissible only when [your principle], which explains the difference between [case set 1] and [case set 2].”

Now write your own one-sentence thesis from that template. Keep it under 35 words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a thesis statement they can use directly, which would amount to writing part of the assignment for them. Per the rules, I must refuse to draft plug-in language, while still supporting them. They have now developed a clear position, so the most helpful minimal scaffolding is to give a thesis formula they must fill in themselves and prompt them to produce their own one-sentence version. This keeps the interaction Socratic, concise, and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: My argument is that act consequentialism is inadequate because it ignores the difference between intended harm and side effects; killing one to save five is permissible only when the death is foreseen, not used as a means.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Clear core claim.

What’s still missing is the case connection: which examples show “foreseen side effect,” and which show “used as a means”? Add one short follow-up sentence naming the categories of cases, in your own words.

Try that next.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a thesis and is likely seeking validation. I should not rewrite or polish it, but I can evaluate it in general terms without grading and push them to strengthen it by connecting it explicitly to the assigned cases. To keep it bite-sized and useful, I’ll affirm the core idea, point out one missing element, and ask for a brief next step.

exchanges.student: The switch-style trolley case and probably Fuel fit the side-effect category, while footbridge and Blood fit the means category. Can you just make that sound a little more polished and academic so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t polish or phrase it for submission.

Your job now is to make it more precise, not prettier. Ask yourself:
1. Why does switch count as side effect?
2. Why does footbridge count as means?
3. What feature makes Fuel closer to switch and Blood closer to footbridge?

Write those reasons in plain language. That’s stronger than “academic-sounding” wording.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for polishing they can submit, which the rules explicitly forbid. I should refuse to edit or suggest language, while still helping them improve content through guided discovery. Since they now have the core distinctions and case mapping, the most useful next step is to prompt them to self-revise for precision and confidence, not prose. I will keep it brief and focused on the assignment.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, the student expresses genuine uncertainty about Blood vs. Fuel. The tutor responds with a decisive test question but does not first probe what specifically is confusing the student about the mechanism — it jumps straight to providing the diagnostic tool rather than asking the student to articulate where their reasoning breaks down. This is a mild instance of skipping diagnostic questioning, though the tutor does push back on the 'safe answer' framing.

The tutor consistently refuses to do the student's work and enforces Socratic constraints throughout, but never explicitly explains why this approach supports learning — e.g., that active reasoning builds retention, develops philosophical skill, or prepares the student for independent argumentation. The rationale is implicit at best.

After the student successfully produces a well-reasoned classification of Blood vs. Fuel in Turn 8 and a strong thesis in Turn 9 — both representing clear improvement from earlier confusion — the tutor moves forward without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the reasoning worked.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well on the core Socratic and anti-ghostwriting dimensions, consistently refusing to supply thesis statements, polished sentences, or case classifications, and instead redirecting the student to reason through each step independently. Dialogue quality and communication were strong throughout. The main deductions are: (1) a mild scaffolding failure in Turn 7 where the tutor skipped diagnostic questioning before providing a decision tool; (2) the tutor never explicitly articulated why the Socratic approach benefits the student's learning; and (3) the tutor missed opportunities to prompt reflection after the student's notable improvements in Turns 8–9. The 1.2.B.a deduction is applied with some uncertainty given the tutor did push back on the 'safe answer' framing before offering the test.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready text, never rewrote student sentences, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in Turn 7, when the student expressed uncertainty about Blood, the tutor immediately supplied a diagnostic test rather than first asking the student to articulate where their reasoning broke down. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because the tutor's rationale for refusing to write for the student was never made explicit in terms of learning benefit, and 2 more points under 1.3.C.a because after the student's clear breakthroughs in Turns 8 and 9, the tutor moved forward without prompting any reflection on what changed. Dialogue and communication quality were strong with no meaningful deductions warranted.
